message.content:
WCAG 2.2 has **three active success criteria** under **Guideline 4.1 Robust**: **4.1.2 Name, Role, Value** (Level A), **4.1.3 Status Messages** (Level AA), and the obsolete **4.1.1 Parsing** is *not* part of WCAG 2.2 conformance because W3C removed it from WCAG 2.2.[7][2]

| SC | Official name | Level | One-line description | axe-core / manual |
|---|---|---:|---|---|
| **4.1.1** | **Parsing** | **A** | Verified that content used valid, well-formed markup so user agents and assistive tech could reliably parse it; **removed/obsolete in WCAG 2.2**.[2][7] | **Not applicable in WCAG 2.2 conformance** |
| **4.1.2** | **Name, Role, Value** | **A** | Verifies that the name, role, and state/value of user interface components can be programmatically determined, so assistive technologies can expose them correctly.[7] | **Partial automation**: some violations can be detected by axe-core, but full verification is **manual** |
| **4.1.3** | **Status Messages** | **AA** | Verifies that status messages can be programmatically determined by assistive technologies without moving focus, so updates like errors, confirmations, and loading states are announced.[7] | **Partial automation**: axe-core can detect some patterns, but meaningful verification is **manual** |

**Why 4.1.1 is excluded in WCAG 2.2:** W3C states that WCAG 2.0 and 2.1 success criteria are “essentially the same in 2.2,” with one exception: **“4.1.1 Parsing is obsolete and removed from WCAG 2.2.”**[2] That means it should not be counted among the active WCAG 2.2 success criteria, even though it still appears in older WCAG 2.0/2.1 materials and training references.[2][7]

For the two active Robust criteria:

- **4.1.2 Name, Role, Value** covers whether custom controls expose a correct accessible name, role, and state/value to assistive technologies.[7]
- **4.1.3 Status Messages** covers whether transient updates are announced to screen readers without requiring the user to move focus, which is important for errors, confirmations, and dynamic content changes.[7]

If you want, I can also provide the **exact W3C wording** for each Robust SC and a **WCAG 2.2-only audit interpretation** of what axe-core can and cannot verify for each one.
